116.question: natural treatments for sleep apnea Are there ways to prevent sleep apnea or treat it naturally? The patient is looking for natural remedies to prevent and treat sleep apnea.
116.answer: There are natural ways to help manage obstructive sleep apnea (OSAS) [PMID:22014867]. Identifying and preventing OSA is important [PMID:30709524]. A holistic approach is pertinent in managing OSA [PMID:30709524]. Conservative approaches, such as weight loss and alcohol and tobacco cessation, are also strongly encouraged [PMID:21603432]. Complementary and integrative approaches to OSA management are varied and, in conjunction with conventional methods, may offer some reduction in

sentence_judgments.sentence: This purpose of this case study is to describe a natural method to help in management of obstructive sleep apnea syndrome (OSAS), which is known to be a common and debilitating condition.
The future of OSA management lies in identifying patients at risk of developing OSA and developing strategy to prevent OSA from taking root.

It involves active screening of patients with OSA and treating them and identifying patients with OSA with high risk of preventable serious morbidity and death and intervening early to prevent these from happening.
A holistic approach is pertinent in managing obstructive sleep apnea (OSA).
Although clinicians should be aware of different treatment modalities and adjunctive measures, proactive management of OSA is as important.
The are no data that support the use of drugs as a therapy for obstructive sleep apnea.
There is a good relief in snoring but the reduction in apnea rate is less successful.
The treatment of choice for sleep-related obstructive breathing disorders is nCPAP.
Medications have thus far been unsuccessful as a treatment option for sleep apnea.
Presently, CPAP is considered the first-line treatment for moderate to severe sleep apnea and one of the first-line treatments for mild disease.
Complementary and integrative approaches to OSA management are varied and, in conjunction with conventional methods, may offer some reduction in the apnea-hypopnea index.
Complementary and integrative approaches to OSA management are varied and, in conjunction with conventional methods, may offer some reduction in the apnea-hypopnea index.
Thus, therapy must be individualized to the patient's desires and the severity of the apnea.
However, conservative approaches, such as weight loss and alcohol and tobacco cessation, are also strongly encouraged in the patient with obstructive sleep apnea.
Obstructive sleep apnea is an underrecognized and underdiagnosed medical condition, with a myriad of negative consequences on patients' health and society as a whole.

118.question: cornea injury healing How long does a minor cornea injury take to heal without medical attention? A patient believes his cornea injury does not need medical attention. The patient expects the injury to heal on its own, but asking for the time at which the eye should heal.
118.answer: Minor corneal injuries usually heal within 48 hours [PMID:25140844]. A focused history and physical exam can help identify these injuries [PMID:25140844]. Most corneal abrasions heal in 24 to 72 hours [PMID:15259527]. Corneal injury activates a carefully orchestrated series of repair processes capable of resolving minor lesions over time [PMID:33200044]. The cornea typically re-epithelializes promptly after trauma [PMID:

sentence_judgments.sentence: Corneal injury activates a carefully orchestrated series of repair processes capable of resolving minor lesions over time, but it often fails to overcome the menace of moderate, severe, and chronic injuries and secondary pathophysiologies that permanently impair vision.

The most serious complications of chemical injuries-persistent corneal edema, neovascularization, scarring/haze, limbal stem cell deficiency, and corneal melting-often manifest over months to years, suggesting that a better understanding of endogenous regenerative mechanisms of corneal repair can lead to the development of improved treatments that may attenuate or prevent corneal defects and protect vision.
Minor corneal abrasions usually heal within 48 hours without complications or eye damage.
A focused history and physical exam can identify patients with corneal abrasions and improve intervention time.
Corneal abrasion is a common eye injury that occurs in all age-groups.
Most corneal abrasions heal in 24 to 72 hours and rarely progress to corneal erosion or infection.
Although eye patching traditionally has been recommended in the treatment of corneal abrasions, multiple well-designed studies show that patching does not help and may hinder healing.
Referral is indicated for any patient with symptoms that do not improve or that worsen, a corneal infiltrate or ulcer, significant vision loss, or a penetrating eye injury.
History and physical examination should exclude serious causes of eye pain, including penetrating injury, infective keratitis, and corneal ulcers.
After trauma, the cornea typically re-epithelializes promptly thereby minimizing the risk of infection, opacification or perforation.
In the case of deeper corneal injury and corneal pathology the wound healing cascade can also extend to the corneal stroma, the layer below the epithelium.
Although significant progress has been made in recent years, pharmaco-therapeutic agents that promote corneal healing remain limited.
The authors review the pathophysiology of perioperative corneal abrasion, and propose updated evidence-based guidelines for improved patient care.

Persistent corneal epithelial defects (PEDs or PCEDs) result from the failure of rapid re-epithelialization and closure within 10-14 days after a corneal injury, even with standard supportive treatment.
Disruptions in the protective epithelial and stromal layers of the cornea can render the eye susceptible to infection, stromal ulceration, perforation, scarring, and significant vision loss.

120.question: causes of facial numbness What causes left sided facial numbness? This patient is feeling the left side of his face is numb. He did not have any injuries or procedures, and feels well otherwise. He is concerned this might be a sign of a problem and wants to know if the causes need medical attention.
120.answer: Left-sided facial numbness can have several causes [PMID:17549061, PMID:16940363, PMID:16264857, PMID:16264857, PMID:30798276, PMID:30798276, PMID:18280947, PMID:18280947, PMID:17305614]. It could be due to a problem with the trig

sentence_judgments.sentence: A 37-year-old woman without important previous medical history presented to an emergency room with acute onset of left-sided weakness and numbness.
We report a case of congenital absence of the left internal carotid artery (ICA) that presented with left-sided facial numbness.
A 37-year-old Brazilian man was admitted to Massachusetts General Hospital for evaluation of left-sided facial numbness, left-sided ataxia, dizziness, and vertigo.
Seven weeks prior to admission, he reported numbness of the left oral cavity and tongue after a dental procedure.
This 37-year-old man presented with left sided facial warmth and numbness associated with new sudden-onset right hemiparesis.
The patient first developed sudden numbness of his left lip and warmth in left ear which travelled to the rest of left face.
We report a case of a 79-year-old woman with widespread metastatic disease secondary to breast carcinoma who presented with a left-sided numb lip and chin.
Numb chin syndrome is almost unknown within the dental and oral and maxillofacial community, despite being well reported in the medical literature.
Facial numbness indicates trigeminal sensory alteration affecting the trigeminal system.
The diagnosis of idiopathic facial paralysis is made after a thorough history and physical examination to exclude alternative etiologies and follow-up to ensure recovery of facial function.

Atypical presentation, recurrent paralysis, additional neurologic deficits, lack of facial recovery in 2-3 months, or a history of head and neck or cutaneous malignancy are concerning for alternative causes of facial paralysis requiring workup.
Facial paralysis is the most common cranial nerve paralysis and the majority of these are idiopathic.
To report a case series of 14 patients presenting with facial numbness primarily to the ophthalmology clinic.
Numb Chin Syndrome (NCS), which is also characterized as sensory neuropathy of the mental nerve, describes a mostly unilateral numbness of the chin and lower lip.
Conclusions Bilateral facial nerve palsy is an extremely rare condition with a varied etiology.
